caveats: a: The occupational source covers broad machinery manufacturing.
a: Engineering and service-diagnostic tasks are only partially substitutable and still need accountable review.

rho: Safety-critical specifications and test results require human verification.

e: The frozen firm count is modeled and may misclassify multi-establishment companies.

s5: Broad transaction data include less technical and more locally oriented manufacturers.
q: Provider claims describe offerings, not realized margins.
q: Core credits and exchange economics complicate comparisons of retained benefit.

o: Remote diagnostics could reduce some service interaction without eliminating physical supply.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Fluid Power Pump and Motor Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is business-to-business and combines recurring OEM production, specification-based replacement units, remanufactured exchange programs, parts, and repair or rebuild jobs. Products are commonly quoted by model or application, with downtime and technical fit supporting aftermarket economics.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure distributors, field-service businesses without manufacturing or remanufacturing capability, general-purpose pump makers, motor-vehicle oil or water pump makers, captive internal shops, and multinational platform-scale suppliers outside the lower middle market.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers and factory remanufacturers of hydraulic or pneumatic fluid-power pumps and motors that supply repeat OEM, replacement, exchange, rebuild, or repair work to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS occupation data measure all three-digit machinery manufacturing, not 333996. I treat office/administrative, engineering, management, business/financial, sales, computer, and information-heavy planning and quality tasks as partially AI-exposed, while excluding most machining, assembly, teardown, cleaning, inspection, test-stand, and material-handling work. Pump and motor manufacturing can be precision-production-intensive, so the broad-industry proxy may overstate exposure.
caveats: The occupational source covers broad machinery manufacturing.
Engineering and service-diagnostic tasks are only partially substitutable and still need accountable review.
a.change_evidence: A 333996-specific occupational matrix or payroll-by-function sample from independent pump and motor manufacturers would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: The most exposed wage-weighted work is quotation, product matching, engineering reuse, service diagnostics, documentation, purchasing, planning, customer support, and administration. Precision remanufacture and new-unit production retain a large physical and skilled-trade component.

d5.bridge: NFPA's shipment forecast covers total fluid power and broad end markets, while Parker's disclosures cover a diversified global supplier, not U.S. LMM pump and motor makers. I translate the 2026 recovery, wide mobile and industrial installed base, and automation or efficiency investment into modest five-year constant-quality growth, offset by machinery cycles, tariff and margin pressure, and partial electrification. A one-year recovery forecast may overstate a normalized five-year outcome.
caveats: The NFPA public figure is a near-term total-fluid-power forecast, not a product-specific five-year projection.
A target concentrated in construction, agriculture, mining, or energy can diverge sharply from the aggregate.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year U.S. hydraulic and pneumatic pump or motor shipment forecasts by end market, paired with target installed-base and customer-concentration data, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Pumps and motors serve recurring replacement, rebuild, industrial, off-highway, transport, and energy demand. Electrified hydraulics and smarter controls can preserve the technology's role, but direct electric drives, end-market cyclicality, and efficiency-driven redesign create downside.

caveats: Public websites often blur distribution, repair, remanufacture, and original manufacturing.
The frozen firm count is modeled and may misclassify multi-establishment companies.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of revenue mix, customer recurrence, corporate affiliation, EBITDA, and manufacturing versus distribution activity would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Repeat OEM parts, installed-base replacements, and repair or exchange programs fit the recurring external-customer lens. Eligibility falls where a firm is captive, dominated by one-off engineering, mainly distribution, outside the EBITDA band despite dataset classification, or an establishment of a larger strategic platform.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333996 ($1,820,201,749) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333996 ($5,667,334,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $447,621 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333996); class <5: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,573,536 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $308,728 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,547,261 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $303,573 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,976,417 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $780,173 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,977,400 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,134,766 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,459,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,703,974 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $211,561,550 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,508,376 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 40

caveats: Technology substitution is captured primarily in demand durability to avoid double counting.
Remote diagnostics could reduce some service interaction without eliminating physical supply.
o.change_evidence: End-user evidence on in-house remanufacture, automated configuration and diagnostics, and direct-electric replacement of fluid-power pumps or motors would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: The delivered service basket requires manufactured or remanufactured pressure-bearing rotating equipment, physical inspection, machining, assembly, and test-stand verification. AI may automate selection, support, and reporting but cannot supply the pump or motor as software; operator displacement is limited mainly to customer self-service and substitution by electric drives.

q.bridge: Aftermarket provider pages document quoted repairs, free evaluations, exchange programs, warranties, OEM-spec testing, rapid turnaround, and downtime-oriented value, but do not measure the share of AI benefits retained. I infer stronger retention in urgent, obsolete, replacement, and remanufactured work and weaker retention in recurring OEM production or widely cross-shopped components. The proxy could overstate retention for targets dominated by contract OEM supply.
caveats: Provider claims describe offerings, not realized margins.
Core credits and exchange economics complicate comparisons of retained benefit.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level repricing terms and gross-margin bridges after measurable quoting, engineering, or administrative productivity gains would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Technical matching, tested performance, availability, warranty, and reduced downtime support value capture. Customers can still compare new, rebuilt, and third-party alternatives, while OEM contracts eventually reprice productivity gains.

caveats: Many targets may lack clean digital service histories and interoperable systems.
Safety-critical specifications and test results require human verification.
rho.change_evidence: Pilot results showing cycle-time and labor changes for quote-to-order, teardown evaluation, parts identification, and technical support at comparable operators would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation is plausible in quote drafting, cross-referencing obsolete models, retrieval of service history, inspection-report drafting, purchasing, scheduling, and customer communications. Fragmented legacy part data, image and measurement quality, warranty exposure, and integration with ERP, drawings, and test records constrain realization.

s5.bridge: Broad manufacturing closed transactions from BizBuySell and economy-wide Census owner-characteristics data do not measure control transfers in 333996. I use them to infer a moderate five-year succession and sale probability, then discount for the industry's 146 employer establishments, strategic ownership, multi-site companies, and specialist buyer universe. The broad small-business marketplace may overstate availability for technical pump and motor manufacturers.
caveats: Establishments are not firms or transferable control assets.
Broad transaction data include less technical and more locally oriented manufacturers.
s5.change_evidence: A proprietary list of independent 333996 firms matched to owner age, ownership type, prior recapitalizations, and closed strategic or sponsor deals would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Founder succession and a functioning manufacturing transaction market create sale flow, but the niche population is exceptionally small and likely contains corporate plants that cannot transfer independently.

principal_driver: A large installed base produces repeated matching, replacement, exchange, and rebuild workflows where proprietary history can make AI operationally valuable.
principal_weakness: Only 146 employer establishments are reported, and many are not independent transferable LMM firms, sharply constraining roll-up supply.
